serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of steamed rice in Singapore is approximately 150 g, equivalent to roughly half a cup of dry rice cooked with water.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Steamed Rice (White Rice, Cooked)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for steamed white rice. The input record carried HPB provenance at confidence 0.95; values are preserved as supplied. Note: protein (6.5 g/100 g), fat (4.21 g/100 g), dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g), and sodium (231 mg/100 g) are notably elevated compared to plain steamed white rice in USDA SR Legacy (protein ~2.7 g, fat ~0.3 g, fibre ~0.4 g, sodium ~1 mg). This suggests the HPB entry may represent an enriched, mixed-grain, or seasoned preparation rather than plain white rice. Values are retained as supplied by the canonical HPB source; downstream users should verify the exact preparation variant.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — White rice, steamed
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is the published mean GI for steamed white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 25.19 × 1.5) / 100 = (72 × 37.79) / 100 ≈ 27.2 for a 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 231 mg/100 g is borderline elevated for a plain rice dish; however, potassium at 327 mg/100 g and dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g provide partial compensatory benefit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should prepare steamed rice without added salt and limit portion size to one bowl (150 g) to keep sodium intake from this dish below 350 mg; the elevated potassium content partially offsets the sodium load and supports blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) and glycemic load per serving (~27) drive rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; carbohydrate at 25.2 g/100 g with a high GI is unfavourable for glycaemic control in Type 2 Diabetes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit steamed white rice to a maximum of half a bowl (75 g cooked) per meal, pair it with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glucose spike, and consider substituting with brown rice, mixed-grain rice, or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic impact, in line with ADA dietary guidance on limiting high-GI refined grains.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g and fat is 4.21 g/100 g; however, the high glycaemic load and elevated sodium (231 mg/100 g) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors, particularly for individuals with metabolic syndrome.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, steamed rice should be consumed in controlled portions (one bowl, 150 g) with sodium-restricted accompaniments; the absence of cholesterol and low saturated fat are favourable, but the high glycaemic load warrants substitution with whole-grain alternatives to reduce cardiometabolic risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed rice (蒸饭; Nasi Kukus) is a foundational staple dish across Singapore and Southeast Asia, prepared by cooking polished white rice grains with water using steam heat until the grains are fully cooked, soft, and slightly sticky. The texture is fluffy yet cohesive, with a mild, neutral flavour that makes it an ideal accompaniment to a wide range of savoury dishes including curries, stir-fries, braised meats, and soups. In Singapore, steamed rice is served at virtually every meal occasion — from hawker centre plates to home-cooked dinners — and forms the caloric backbone of dishes such as Hainanese chicken rice, char siew rice, and economy rice. Nutritionally, this HPB-sourced entry reports 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g and sodium at 231 mg/100 g — values that are notably higher than plain white rice in global databases, suggesting this entry may represent an enriched, mixed-grain, or lightly seasoned preparation. The glycemic index of steamed white rice is high (GI ~72), making portion control critical for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Potassium at 327 mg/100 g and magnesium at 40 mg/100 g contribute to electrolyte balance. Steamed rice is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher, making it suitable across diverse dietary requirements. Clinically, it is best consumed in controlled portions paired with protein and fibre-rich vegetables to moderate postprandial glycaemic response.

Is steamed rice good for diabetics?
Can I eat steamed rice with hypertension?
Steamed rice vs brown rice nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with input confidence 0.95 and provenance marked canonical. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. (2008). Multilingual names verified for Singaporean cuisine category. Minor caveat: the HPB entry's macronutrient profile (protein 6.5 g, fat 4.21 g, fibre 3.7 g, sodium 231 mg per 100 g) is atypical for plain steamed white rice and may reflect a mixed-grain or enriched preparation; this is documented in notes.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific steamed rice record; these values are not available in the Singapore HPB database as accessed 2026-04-16. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source — steamed white rice contains no dietary cholesterol, which is naturally zero for a plant-based grain food. 3. The HPB entry reports protein 6.5 g/100 g, fat 4.21 g/100 g, dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g, and sodium 231 mg/100 g — all substantially higher than USDA SR Legacy values for plain cooked white rice (protein ~2.7 g, fat ~0.3 g, fibre ~0.4 g, sodium ~1 mg per 100 g). This discrepancy strongly suggests the HPB entry represents a mixed-grain, enriched, or lightly seasoned rice preparation rather than plain polished white rice. Values are retained as supplied by the canonical HPB source; downstream users should verify the exact preparation variant. 4. Input cultural.primary_cuisine was listed as 'Western', which is inconsistent with steamed rice being a pan-Asian staple; cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean / Pan-Asian' for accuracy. 5. GI of 72 applies to plain steamed white rice; if this entry represents a mixed-grain preparation, the actual GI may be lower (brown rice GI ~50–55; mixed grain GI ~55–65).
